began on April 14, 1942. The regular season ended on September 27, with the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals") and [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") as the regular season champions of the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") and [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League"), respectively. The postseason began with Game 1 of the [39th World Series](/wiki/1942_World_Series "1942 World Series") on September 30 and ended with Game 5 on October 5. In the third iteration of this World Series matchup, the Cardinals defeated the Yankees, four games to one, capturing their fourth championship in franchise history, since their previous in [1934](/wiki/1934_Major_League_Baseball_season "1934 Major League Baseball season"). Going into the season, the defending [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") champions were the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") from the [1941](/wiki/1941_Major_League_Baseball_season "1941 Major League Baseball season") season.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/ButtonBlue.svg/20px-ButtonBlue.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ButtonBlue.svg) [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)")

The **1942 major league baseball season** began on April 14, 1942. The regular season ended on September 27, with the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals") and [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") as the regular season champions of the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") and [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League"), respectively. The postseason began with Game 1 of the [39th World Series](/wiki/1942_World_Series "1942 World Series") on September 30 and ended with Game 5 on October 5. In the third iteration of this World Series matchup, the Cardinals defeated the Yankees, four games to one, capturing their fourth championship in franchise history, since their previous in [1934](/wiki/1934_Major_League_Baseball_season "1934 Major League Baseball season"). Going into the season, the defending [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") champions were the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") from the [1941](/wiki/1941_Major_League_Baseball_season "1941 Major League Baseball season") season.

The [10th Major League Baseball All-Star Game](/wiki/1942_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "1942 Major League Baseball All-Star Game") was played on July 6 at the [Polo Grounds](/wiki/Polo_Grounds "Polo Grounds") in [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), home of the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants_(baseball) "New York Giants (baseball)"). The [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") won, 3–1.

In the National League, the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers") had a record of 104–50, but finished two [games behind](/wiki/Games_behind "Games behind") the Cardinals; the Dodgers tied the [1909 Chicago Cubs](/wiki/1909_Chicago_Cubs_season "1909 Chicago Cubs season"), who had a record of 104–49, for the most wins in an MLB regular season without reaching the postseason.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/Philadelphia_Athletics "Philadelphia Athletics") set a record for the fewest [runs batted in](/wiki/Runs_batted_in "Runs batted in") during a season, with only 354.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The [St. Louis Browns](/wiki/St._Louis_Browns "St. Louis Browns") nearly moved to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [California](/wiki/California "California") for the start of the 1942 season. During the 1941 [Winter Meetings](/wiki/Winter_Meetings "Winter Meetings") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), a vote was scheduled for the morning of December 8, and was expected to be approved. However, due to the [attack on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor") by [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") the previous day (and subsequent [entry of the United States](/wiki/United_States_in_World_War_II "United States in World War II") into [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")), when it came time to vote, all teams (including the Browns) unanimously voted against the move.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) The team would eventually leave for [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") in [1954](/wiki/1954_Major_League_Baseball_season "1954 Major League Baseball season") where they remain today as the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles"), while Los Angeles would eventually get a major league team in [1958](/wiki/1958_Major_League_Baseball_season "1958 Major League Baseball season") when the [Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers") moved from [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)").

## Schedule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1942_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Schedule")]

See also: [Major League Baseball schedule](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_schedule "Major League Baseball schedule")

The 1942 schedule consisted of 154 games for all teams in the American League and National League, each of which had eight teams. Each team was scheduled to play 22 games against the other seven teams of their respective league. This continued the format put in place since the [1904](/wiki/1904_Major_League_Baseball_season "1904 Major League Baseball season") season (except for [1919](/wiki/1919_Major_League_Baseball_season "1919 Major League Baseball season")) and would be used until [1961](/wiki/1961_Major_League_Baseball_season "1961 Major League Baseball season") in the American League and [1962](/wiki/1962_Major_League_Baseball_season "1962 Major League Baseball season") in the National League.

Opening Day, April 16, featured all sixteen teams, the first since the [1940](/wiki/1940_Major_League_Baseball_season "1940 Major League Baseball season") season. The final day of the regular season was on September 27 and featured ten teams. The [World Series](/wiki/1942_World_Series "1942 World Series") took place between September 30 and October 5.

## Rule changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1942_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Rule changes")]

The 1942 season saw the following rule changes:

* Any player [drafted](/wiki/Conscription_in_the_United_States#World_War_II "Conscription in the United States") for [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") would be regarded as voluntarily retired, though placed on a separate list.[[5]](#cite_note-1941_Winter_SABR-5)
* Regarding [doubleheaders](/wiki/Doubleheader_(baseball) "Doubleheader (baseball)"):
  + It was made official that both St. Louis teams could schedule doubleheaders after the third Sunday home game (as opposed to the other fourteen teams, who must wait until after the fourth home Sunday game).[[5]](#cite_note-1941_Winter_SABR-5)
  + Regularly scheduled games could not be pushed back to the final series of the season for the purpose of causing a doubleheader.[[5]](#cite_note-1941_Winter_SABR-5)
* If the postponement of a contest requires transfer between cities, a game cannot be called off less than one hour before the game’s scheduled start time.[[5]](#cite_note-1941_Winter_SABR-5)
* In the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League"), a rule that was previously implemented for the [1940](/wiki/1940_Major_League_Baseball_season "1940 Major League Baseball season") season, which limited a defending champion team from trading with other American League teams, except through [waivers](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_transactions#Waivers "Major League Baseball transactions"), was repealed.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* The number of [night games](/wiki/Night_game#Baseball "Night game") allowed per team was increased from 7 to 14 (with the [Washington Senators](/wiki/Washington_Senators_(1901%E2%80%931960) "Washington Senators (1901–1960)") allowed 21).[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1942_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Venues")]

Over 80 home games, the [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/Cleveland_Guardians "Cleveland Guardians") played 46 games at Cleveland Stadium and 34 games at League Park.[[35]](#cite_note-35) All Thursday home games took place at League Park. This would be the 8th of 12 seasons since [1932](/wiki/1932_Major_League_Baseball_season "1932 Major League Baseball season") that saw the Indians play at both venues.

Following the reversion of the Boston Bees name to [Boston Braves](/wiki/Boston_Braves "Boston Braves") the [previous season](/wiki/1941_Major_League_Baseball_season "1941 Major League Baseball season"), National League Park's name was also reverted, to [Braves Field](/wiki/Braves_Field "Braves Field").

## Retired numbers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1942_Major_League_Baseball_season&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Retired numbers")]

The [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds") re-entered [Willard Hershberger](/wiki/Willard_Hershberger "Willard Hershberger")'s No. 5 into circulation, only [two years after](/wiki/1940_Major_League_Baseball_season "1940 Major League Baseball season") his number was retired. This was the first number to be de-retired by any team in MLB. His number would later be re-retired to honor [Johnny Bench](/wiki/Johnny_Bench "Johnny Bench") in [1984](/wiki/1984_Major_League_Baseball_season "1984 Major League Baseball season").
